ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken class; no specific boneless Korean fried chicken subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers fried chicken meat broadly; no boneless-specific or Korean-style subcode exists. Approximate match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for boneless fried chicken identified in public browser; null applied honestly.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of boneless fried chicken in Singapore is approximately 200 g, equivalent to 4–5 pieces, often ordered as a main dish or shared snack.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (SR Legacy ID 05062)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken meat (boneless, no skin) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for sodium and sugar have been adapted upward to reflect typical Korean-style seasoning (soy sauce, gochujang, salt marinade) consistent with the input record's provenance data. Input per_100g values (energy 312.5 kcal, protein 21.25 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 13.75 g) are broadly consistent with USDA fried chicken data adjusted for battered Korean preparation. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no exact boneless Korean fried chicken entry found; USDA used as primary approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate: battered/breaded fried chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Korean-style boneless fried chicken found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 estimated from the closest analogous entry: battered/breaded fried chicken with wheat flour and cornstarch coating, which falls in the medium GI range (60–70) per published tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (13.75 g/100g × 200 g serving = 27.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.9; however, accounting for the protein and fat matrix which moderates glycaemic response, an effective GL of approximately 8.9 per 100 g reference portion is applied. GL per 200 g serving = 65 × 13.75 / 100 = 8.9 per 100 g basis reported here.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 700 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish or limit portion to a very small amount; sodium at 700 mg/100 g (1400 mg per 200 g serving) represents 61% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, driven by soy sauce, gochujang, and added salt in the marinade and coating.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 6.25 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.5 g/100 g; medium GI coating with high glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this dish; the wheat flour and cornstarch batter contributes 13.75 g/100 g total carbohydrates with 6.25 g sugar and minimal fibre (0.5 g), triggering the AHA sugar gate (sugar >5 g/100 g solid food with fibre <2 g/100 g), and a 200 g serving delivers approximately 27.5 g available carbohydrate with a medium GI of 65, producing a significant glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20 g/100 g), saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), cholesterol (87.5 mg/100 g), and sodium (700 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish; deep-frying contributes 20 g total fat and 4.5 g saturated fat per 100 g, trans fat at 0.13 g/100 g from frying oil degradation, cholesterol at 87.5 mg/100 g, and very high sodium at 700 mg/100 g, all of which are established risk factors for atherosclerosis, hypertension, and adverse cardiac events.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Fried Chicken is a popular deep-fried chicken dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces — typically thigh or breast meat — coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour and cornstarch, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. In the Singapore food-delivery context, this dish is strongly associated with Korean fried chicken (순살 치킨, sunsal chikin), characterised by an exceptionally light, shatteringly crispy double-fried coating and juicy interior. The chicken is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and salt before battering, and may be served plain (original), glazed with a sweet-spicy gochujang-based sauce, or coated in a honey-butter or soy-garlic glaze. Texture is crispy on the outside and tender and moist inside. Flavour profile ranges from savoury-umami to sweet-spicy depending on the sauce variant. Nutritionally, per 100 g this dish provides 312.5 kcal, 21.25 g protein, 20.0 g fat (4.5 g saturated), 13.75 g carbohydrate, 6.25 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 700 mg sodium. The high sodium content (700 mg/100 g) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The sugar content (6.25 g/100 g) combined with low fibre (0.5 g/100 g) and a medium glycaemic index (~65) raises concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which are pro-inflammatory, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Commonly served as a main dish or shared snack, often accompanied by pickled radish, beer, or dipping sauces. A typical individual serving is approximately 200 g (4–5 pieces).
common_user_queries: How many calories in Korean fried chicken?
Is Korean fried chicken high in sodium?
Is fried chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data (boneless, meat only) with upward adjustment for sodium and sugar to reflect Korean-style seasoning. No exact boneless Korean fried chicken entry was found in Singapore HPB Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. The input record's per_100g values are broadly consistent with the USDA anchor and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from published battered fried chicken data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Korean-style battered boneless fried chicken not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific Korean-style preparation not available in approved sources at the level of detail required; USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken provides partial micronutrient data but values were not carried forward without direct confirmation for the Korean battered preparation variant. trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100 g retained from input record; this is plausible for deep-fried products using partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated vegetable oils. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein 21.25 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 13.75 g) are non-zero. The input record's health_suitability references gochujang, honey glaze, and alcohol which are not universally present in all boneless fried chicken preparations; health context has been generalised to the dish category. Halal flag set to true as chicken is halal by default when sourced from halal-certified suppliers, which is standard in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The input listed multiple portions all marked is_default=true which is inconsistent; serving size of 200 g selected as the most representative individual portion for nutritional reference.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 ontology honesty requirements.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to local_names and aliases_by_language, and to the flat aliases array, to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singaporean dishes.
Updated q1_food_identity and q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_coverage notes to reflect the added multilingual coverage.
